items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: Which of these is NOT usually a sign of a chemical reaction?
items.why: It's Ice melting. “Bubbles of gas” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A reaction that releases heat is called:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Exothermic. “Endothermic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a beaker temperature rises from 20°C to 35°C during a reaction, by how many °C did it rise?
items.why: The real figure is 15. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
